Atty. KIMBERLY BLACK, Esq. of Koeller, Nebeker, Carlson & Haluck, LLP (Tel: 916-724-5700) for cross-defendant Storage San Francisco, LLC.

HEARING RE: CROSS-DEFENDANT STATION 1 FIRE PROTECTION's Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement Pursuant To CCP 877.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

"Station 1 Fire Protection's motion for determination of good faith settlement pursuant to CCP 877" is granted.

Plaintiff alleges he was injured by stepping on a vault lid that gave way. At least six parties (including plaintiff) have potential culpability. (Memo. 5:20-6:2.) One of them, Harrigan Weidenmuller Co., filed an opposition to the good faith motion.
Harrigan focuses on its notion that not enough discovery has been done to allow a good faith settlement. (Opp. 2:26-3:19, 3:28-4:17, 4:23-5:4.) However, CCP 877 motions are often granted early in litigation (Cahill v. San Diego Gas & Electric Co. (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 939) and even before suit is filed (County of Los Angeles v. Guerrero (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 1149, 1152-55). This is sensical, as a primary goal of section 877 is to avoid discovery costs.

Harrigan rightly notes that the CCP 877 evaluation is "made on the basis of information available at the time of settlement." (Opp. 2:16-17, citing Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499.) Here, that information - set out in Station 1's thorough memorandum - suffices to enable "a rough estimation of plaintiff['s] total recovery and the settlor's proportionate liability." (Id.) The $5,000 settlement is in good faith because the available evidence indicates little or no liability by Station 1 and largely speculative damages for plaintiff. (Memo. 6:4-11:4.)

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Sharee Hess, Esq., KOELLER, NEBEKER, CARLSON & HALUCK, LLP, (916) 788-2850, sharee.hess@knchlaw.com, for Cross-Defendant STORAGE SAN FRANCISCO, LLC.
Christine Fenech, Esq., LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP, 415.362.2580, christine.fench@lewisbrisbois.com, for Defendant/Cross-Complainant HARRIGAN, WEIDENMULLER CO., incorrectly named as HARRIGAN WEIDENMULLER COMPANY

Hearing is held. Argued. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT STORAGE SAN FRANCISCO, LLC DEMURRER to COMPLAINT

Storage San Francisco, LLC's "demurrer to plaintiff Michael Kurpieski's complaint" - joined by Harrigan, Weidenmuller Co. - is overruled.

The complaint pleads that "[a]t, near, or adjacent to 435 23rd Street in San Francisco, Plaintiff was injured when he stepped on a metal access plate on the ground adjacent to a fire department connection." It appears undisputed that HWC owned, and SSF leased, 435 23rd Street and that the fire department connection served that property. (Memo. 3:19-24; Opp. 1:5-15; Villeggiante Dec. Ex. 3i.) The demurrer says the connection was actually located on adjoining property at 420 23rd Street, but that matters not. As plaintiff points out, landowners may be liable for conditions on adjoining land that are related to their property. (Alcaraz v. Vece (1997) 14 Cal.4th 1149. 1161-62; Alpert v. Villa Romano Homeowners Association (2000) 18 Cal.App.4th 1320, 1334-37; Moeller v. Fleming (136 CalApp.3d (1982) 241, 244-45.)

The complaint is pled broadly enough to embrace plaintiff's theory. The parties' other arguments, requests and objections need not be addressed.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling order in compliance with CRC3.1312.
